g.data-narration: Welcome to serializability in databases. We explore how concurrent transactions maintain data consistency through serializability theory.
Concurrent transactions improve performance but can cause data inconsistencies. Serializability ensures correct execution equivalent to serial processing.
g.text: • Concurrent transactions improve throughput but risk data inconsistency
• Lost updates, uncommitted data, and inconsistent states can occur
• Serializability guarantees results equivalent to some serial execution

g.data-narration: A transaction is a logical unit transforming database state. A schedule is a chronological sequence of operations from multiple transactions.
g.text: A logical unit of work that

g.data-narration: A serial schedule executes transactions one after another without interleaving, ensuring consistency but limiting concurrency.
g.text: Transactions execute one after another with NO interleaving
Allows interleaving of operations from different transactions

g.data-narration: Three conflict types exist: Read-Write, Write-Read, and Write-Write. Conflicts occur when operations access the same data item with at least one write.
g.text: Two operations conflict if they access same data item AND at least one is a write

g.data-narration: A schedule is conflict serializable if it can be transformed into a serial schedule by swapping non-conflicting operations. Swapping preserves final outcomes.
g.text: A schedule is conflict serializable if it can be transformed into a SERIAL schedule
by swapping pairs of NON-CONFLICTING operations
Key: Swapping non-conflicting ops doesn't change final outcome

g.data-narration: The precedence graph tests conflict serializability using nodes for transactions and directed edges for conflicting operations.
g.text: Directed graph used to TEST conflict serializability
• Nodes: Each transaction in the schedule
• Edge Ti→Tj: Ti has conflicting op BEFORE Tj's op
Acyclic → topological ordering gives equivalent serial schedule

g.data-narration: Example 1 shows a conflict-serializable schedule. The precedence graph has nodes T1 and T2 with edge T1→T2, no cycle exists.
Example 2 demonstrates a non-serializable schedule with a cycle in the precedence graph. The edges T1→T2 and T2→T1 form a cycle.
View serializability is broader than conflict serializability. It allows blind writes but testing for it is NP-complete.
g.text: Broader than conflict serializability - schedule is view-equivalent to some serial schedule
1. Same initial reads 2. Same writes 3. Same final writes
• Every conflict serializable schedule IS view-serializable
• But NOT vice versa (blind writes create counterexamples)

g.data-narration: A recoverable schedule ensures a transaction commits only after all transactions whose changes it read have committed.
g.text: Transaction commits only AFTER all transactions whose changes it READ have committed
NON-RECOVERABLE: T1:R(X) W(X) Abort | T2: R(X) Commit
T2 reads X from T1, but T1 aborts while T2 commits!
RECOVERABLE: T1:R(X) W(X) Commit | T2: R(X) Commit
T2 waits for T1 to commit before committing itself

g.data-narration: Cascadeless schedules require transactions to read only values from committed transactions, preventing cascading rollbacks.
g.text: Transaction reads ONLY values written by COMMITTED transactions
Strict subset of recoverable: Cascadeless ⇒ Recoverable
but limits concurrency more than recoverable

g.data-narration: The schedule hierarchy shows strict schedules as most restrictive, followed by cascadeless, then recoverable, then all schedules.
Remember these key formulas: conflict serializable iff acyclic graph, cascadeless is subset of recoverable, three conflict types exist.
g.text: • Schedule conflict serializable ⇔ Precedence graph acyclic
